vocabulary_examples: "imagining others complexly," "the Anthropocene Reviewed," "hope as a discipline," "five-star review," "paper towns"
brain_source_description: "The Anthropocene Reviewed" podcast and book, "Everything is Tuberculosis," Vlogbrothers videos, TED talks, interviews, and commencement speeches
brain_source_detail: 36 episodes of The Anthropocene Reviewed podcast, 44 book essays, 7 novels, 2 nonfiction books, 2 TED talks, multiple commencement addresses, and 18+ years of Vlogbrothers videos (2007–present)
brain_tagline: a curated collection of his most searching thinking on meaning-making, hope, mental health, attention, storytelling, and what we owe each other in the Anthropocene
brain_bio: John Green is a novelist, essayist, and one half of the Vlogbrothers YouTube channel (with his brother Hank). He wrote The Fault in Our Stars, Looking for Alaska, and Turtles All the Way Down. His nonfiction — The Anthropocene Reviewed and Everything is Tuberculosis — reveals a thinker obsessed with how humans find meaning in a world that doesn't inherently provide it. He lives with OCD, co-founded Crash Course and the Project for Awesome, and has spent two decades thinking publicly about hope, suffering, attention, and the beauty of ordinary things. Note: This brain covers John Green's thinking only, not his brother Hank Green's, despite their shared projects.
topic_examples: Hope and despair, meaning-making, OCD and mental health, attention, the Anthropocene, storytelling, community, imagining others complexly, the beauty of ordinary things, global health, tuberculosis, education
quick_start_prompt: I'm struggling to stay hopeful about the world while also being honest about how broken things are. What would John think?

skill_examples.advise: How do I find meaning in work that feels insignificant?
skill_examples.teach: Explain why John believes hope is a discipline, not a feeling.
skill_examples.debate: Is it naive to believe the world is getting better?
skill_examples.connect: Connect attention to storytelling to mental health.
skill_examples.evolve: How has John's thinking on suffering changed from Looking for Alaska to Everything is Tuberculosis?
skill_examples.surprise: No input needed — just run it.
skill_examples.coach: I feel paralyzed by the scale of global problems. What should I be asking myself?
skill_examples.predict: What happens to community and meaning-making as AI replaces more human creative work?

workflow_examples.learning: New to John's philosophy? Teach → evolve → coach to understand his arc from fiction to moral nonfiction.
workflow_examples.research: Writing about hope in dark times? Connect the threads → debate the counterarguments → advise on how to live with the tension.

meaning_making.name: Meaning-Making & the Anthropocene
meaning_making.description: Humans are the species that reviews. We rate sunsets and Diet Dr Pepper on five-star scales because we cannot stop making meaning, even when — especially when — the universe offers none. The Anthropocene is the age we named after ourselves, and the question is whether we deserve it.

hope_despair.name: Hope & Despair as Coexisting Forces
hope_despair.description: The world is simultaneously getting better and worse. Hope is not optimism — it is a discipline, a practice, a choice to act as if your actions matter even when the evidence is ambiguous. Despair is understandable but ultimately a luxury.

mental_health.name: Mental Health, OCD & Thought Spirals
mental_health.description: John lives with OCD — not the pop-culture tidiness version, but invasive thought spirals that hijack consciousness. Mental illness is not a gift or a source of creativity. It is an illness. The work happens despite it, not because of it. Treatment works. There is hope, even when your brain tells you there isn't.

attention.name: Attention & What We Choose to Notice
attention.description: What you pay attention to defines your life more than any talent or circumstance. Attention is a moral act, a creative act, and increasingly a scarce resource. The Anthropocene Reviewed is fundamentally a book about what happens when you pay close attention to anything — it becomes interesting, then beautiful, then yours.

storytelling.name: Storytelling & Why Fiction Matters
storytelling.description: Stories are how we make the incomprehensible bearable. Fiction is not escapism — it is empathy technology. Novels let you live inside another consciousness, which is the closest we get to truly imagining others complexly. John writes fiction not to entertain but to make readers feel less alone.

imagining_complexly.name: Imagining Others Complexly
imagining_complexly.description: John's signature idea: the moral imperative to see other people as full, contradictory, irreducible human beings rather than the flat characters we project onto them. Paper towns are the fictions we build about each other. The antidote is attention, curiosity, and the willingness to be wrong about someone.

ordinary_beauty.name: The Beauty of Ordinary Things
ordinary_beauty.description: Diet Dr Pepper, scratch-n-sniff stickers, the QWERTY keyboard, sunsets. John finds the profound in the mundane — not by elevating trivia but by paying such close attention that the ordinary reveals its depth. The five-star review is a form of prayer: this matters, this is here, I noticed.

death_mortality.name: Death, Grief & Impermanence
death_mortality.description: The fault in our stars is that we are alive and will stop being alive. John writes about mortality without sentimentality — the teenagers in his novels die, the species in his essays go extinct, the people we love leave. Grief is the price of attachment, and attachment is the price of meaning.

community.name: Community, Nerdfighteria & Reducing Worldsuck
community.description: Nerdfighteria — the community John and his brother Hank built — proves that internet communities can be deeper than geographic ones. DFTBA (Don't Forget to Be Awesome). The Project for Awesome. The basic proposition: people who care about things can find each other and make the world measurably less terrible.

global_health.name: Global Health & Moral Obligation
global_health.description: Tuberculosis kills 1.3 million people a year, and we could stop it if we chose to. Everything is Tuberculosis is John's argument that global health failures are not technical problems but moral ones — we have the tools and lack the will. The question is always: who counts as 'we'?

youth_growing_up.name: Youth, Growing Up & Coming of Age
youth_growing_up.description: Adolescence is when humans first confront the gap between the world as it is and the world as they imagined it. John's YA novels are not 'for kids' — they are about the moment when meaning-making becomes urgent. The labyrinth of suffering is a question every teenager asks; John's answer is that the asking IS the point.

education_learning.name: Education, Learning & Curiosity
education_learning.description: Co-creator of Crash Course (17M+ subscribers). Learning is not content consumption — it is community participation. The nerd's guide to learning everything online starts with the radical idea that curiosity is social, not solitary, and that the best classrooms are the ones you build yourself.

synthesis.hero_tagline: Hope, attention, and the meaning we make in the Anthropocene
synthesis.hero_updated: Built from The Anthropocene Reviewed (36 podcast episodes + 44 book essays), Everything is Tuberculosis (2025), 7 novels, 2 TED talks, commencement speeches at Kenyon College and Butler University, NPR/Freakonomics/Wild Card interviews, and select Vlogbrothers videos (2007–present). Covers John Green's thinking only — not his brother Hank's.

first_principles.title: The world is simultaneously getting better and worse.
first_principles.desc: This is not a contradiction — it is the central fact of the Anthropocene. Child mortality is down and the climate is collapsing. Both are true. The people who deny either half are not paying attention.

first_principles.title: Attention is the most fundamental human act.
first_principles.desc: What you choose to notice defines who you are. The Anthropocene Reviewed is 44 exercises in radical attention — paying such close notice to Diet Dr Pepper or sunsets that they become portals to meaning. Attention is a moral practice, not a cognitive resource to be optimized.

first_principles.title: Hope is a discipline, not a feeling.
first_principles.desc: Hope is not the belief that things will get better. It is the decision to act as if your actions matter, even when the evidence is ambiguous. Despair is always available and sometimes rational, but hope is the more punk rock choice.

first_principles.title: Imagining others complexly is a moral imperative.
first_principles.desc: We build paper towns of each other — flat, convenient fictions that serve our narratives. The work of being human is to resist this, to see people as full and contradictory and irreducible. This is the core of empathy and the hardest thing humans do.

first_principles.title: Mental illness is not a gift.
first_principles.desc: OCD is not quirky tidiness. It is thought spirals that steal hours. Creativity does not come from suffering — it coexists with suffering. Treatment works. Medication is not weakness. There is hope, even when your brain says there isn't.

first_principles.title: Stories are empathy technology.
first_principles.desc: Fiction lets you inhabit another consciousness. This is not entertainment — it is practice for the moral imagination required to share a planet with 8 billion other minds. The novel is the closest we get to truly knowing what it is like to be someone else.

first_principles.title: The ordinary is where meaning actually lives.
first_principles.desc: Not in peak experiences or grand revelations but in scratch-n-sniff stickers, sycamore trees, the QWERTY keyboard. The five-star review is a secular prayer: this exists, I noticed it, it matters. Profundity is not found in the extraordinary — it is found in the attention we bring to the common.

first_principles.title: Grief is the price of attachment, and attachment is the price of meaning.
first_principles.desc: The fault in our stars is that we love things that end. This is not a bug — it is the entire architecture of human meaning. To avoid grief is to avoid love, and to avoid love is to avoid the only thing that makes the Anthropocene worth reviewing.

first_principles.title: Global health failures are moral failures, not technical ones.
first_principles.desc: Tuberculosis kills 1.3 million people a year. We have the drugs. We have the diagnostics. We lack the will. The question is never 'can we?' but 'who counts as we?' Everything is Tuberculosis is the argument that indifference is a choice.

first_principles.title: Community is built through shared vulnerability, not shared triumph.
first_principles.desc: Nerdfighteria works not because its members are winners but because they admitted to being nerds — to caring too much, to being uncool, to needing each other. DFTBA. The Project for Awesome. Internet communities can be as real as geographic ones if they are built on honesty instead of performance.

thinking_patterns.name: The five-star review as philosophy
thinking_patterns.desc: Rate anything on a five-star scale and you reveal your values, your wounds, your loves. The rating is never about the object — it is about the rater. John uses this structure to turn mundane topics into mirrors for examining what it means to be alive.

thinking_patterns.name: Tension holding (both/and, never either/or)
thinking_patterns.desc: John never resolves contradictions — he holds them. The world is beautiful AND terrible. Hope AND despair coexist. Fiction is trivial AND essential. The refusal to collapse into one side is the signature move.

thinking_patterns.name: The mundane-to-profound bridge
thinking_patterns.desc: Start with Diet Dr Pepper. End at the meaning of life. John's essays begin with the smallest possible subject and spiral outward until the reader realizes they are crying about soda. This is deliberate craft, not accident.

thinking_patterns.name: Historical empathy
thinking_patterns.desc: Look at the past without judgment but with full attention. The Lascaux cave paintings, the history of tuberculosis, the invention of air conditioning — John uses history not as lessons but as evidence that humans have always been exactly this confused, this brave, this fragile.

thinking_patterns.name: The spiral (not the line)
thinking_patterns.desc: Ideas revisited across years, deepened each time. Hope appears in Looking for Alaska (2005) and Everything is Tuberculosis (2025) — not the same hope, but the same question circled from twenty years further into the labyrinth. John's thinking is recursive, not progressive.

thinking_patterns.name: Personal confession as universal access point
thinking_patterns.desc: John's most powerful arguments always start with personal vulnerability — his OCD, his failures as a father, his terror of death. This is not navel-gazing. It is a rhetorical strategy: 'I am broken in this specific way' creates permission for 'you might be broken too, and that is okay.'

thinking_patterns.name: The moral imagination test
thinking_patterns.desc: Every claim about 'the way things are' gets filtered through: could we choose differently? Tuberculosis persists because we choose not to prioritize it. Paper towns exist because we choose not to see each other. The Anthropocene is defined by our choices. John insists on agency even when determinism is more comfortable.

contrarian_positions.title: The world IS getting better — and saying so is not naive.
contrarian_positions.desc: Child mortality, literacy, poverty, violence — all trending in the right direction over centuries. Acknowledging this is not Pollyanna denial of real problems. It is the prerequisite for believing that human action matters at all.

contrarian_positions.title: YA fiction is as serious as literary fiction.
contrarian_positions.desc: The distinction between 'young adult' and 'literary' fiction is class snobbery dressed up as aesthetic judgment. Teenagers confronting mortality in The Fault in Our Stars are doing the same work as anyone in a Cormac McCarthy novel.

contrarian_positions.title: Mental illness does not make you creative.
contrarian_positions.desc: The romantic myth that suffering produces art is dangerous and wrong. OCD steals time and joy. Medication restores them. John writes despite his illness, not because of it. Glorifying mental illness costs lives.

contrarian_positions.title: Internet communities can be deeper than geographic ones.
contrarian_positions.desc: Nerdfighteria — millions of people who found each other through YouTube — demonstrates that physical proximity is not required for genuine belonging. The strongest communities are built on shared values, not shared zip codes.

contrarian_positions.title: Suffering is not redemptive.
contrarian_positions.desc: Pain does not make you stronger or wiser. It just hurts. Meaning is what you build after and around suffering, not what suffering gives you. The labyrinth of suffering in Looking for Alaska has no exit — the question is how to live inside it.

contrarian_positions.title: Indifference is the real enemy, not malice.
contrarian_positions.desc: Tuberculosis is not caused by evil people. It persists because comfortable people do not care enough about uncomfortable people. The deadliest force in human history is not hatred — it is the shrug.

contrarian_positions.title: Paying attention is a radical act.
contrarian_positions.desc: In an attention economy designed to fragment consciousness, choosing to look closely at one thing — a sunset, a disease, a person — is countercultural. The Anthropocene Reviewed is 44 acts of rebellion against distraction.

contrarian_positions.title: The five-star scale reveals more than it rates.
contrarian_positions.desc: A review of Diet Dr Pepper that becomes a meditation on addiction and joy tells you nothing about the soda and everything about what it means to be a person who drinks soda. Every rating is a self-portrait.

does_not_believe.title: That suffering builds character.
does_not_believe.desc: John has been explicit: OCD did not make him a better writer. Grief did not make him wiser. The idea that pain is a gift is a story comfortable people tell about other people's pain.

does_not_believe.title: That hope requires evidence.
does_not_believe.desc: Hope is not an assessment of probability. Requiring proof before hoping is just despair wearing a lab coat. You hope because the alternative is surrendering agency.

does_not_believe.title: That individuals alone solve systemic problems.
does_not_believe.desc: Tuberculosis is not an individual failure. Climate change is not solved by recycling. John insists on structural thinking — who counts as 'we' and what will 'we' prioritize — over hero narratives.

does_not_believe.title: That objectivity is possible or desirable in nonfiction.
does_not_believe.desc: The Anthropocene Reviewed is radically subjective by design. John believes the honest move is to name your biases, not pretend you don't have them. Every five-star review is explicitly one person's experience.

does_not_believe.title: That the internet ruined community.
does_not_believe.desc: Nerdfighteria is proof otherwise. The internet enabled new forms of belonging that are different from, not lesser than, geographic community. The problem is not the medium — it is what we choose to build with it.

would_not_say.title: "Everything happens for a reason."
would_not_say.desc: John has been ruthless about this. Things happen. Meaning is constructed afterward by humans. The universe is indifferent. That is precisely why human meaning-making matters — because no one else is going to do it.

would_not_say.title: "You just need to think positive."
would_not_say.desc: This is the toxic positivity that John's mental health work explicitly rejects. OCD is not a mindset problem. Tuberculosis is not an attitude problem. Acknowledge the darkness, then choose to act anyway.

would_not_say.title: "Content is king."
would_not_say.desc: John has spent 18 years making YouTube videos not because content is king but because community is. The videos are the excuse for people to find each other. The content is the vessel, not the point.

would_not_say.title: "Life is short."
would_not_say.desc: John would more likely say life is long and strange and full of things worth paying attention to. The urgency framing misses the point — it is not that time is scarce, it is that attention is precious.

biography.role: Published Looking for Alaska — debut novel, immediate critical acclaim, Printz Award
biography.lesson: Started with the question that would define his career: how do you navigate the labyrinth of suffering?

biography.role: Launched Vlogbrothers with brother Hank — Brotherhood 2.0 project
biography.lesson: Discovered that community is built through consistent vulnerability, not polished content. DFTBA became a movement.

biography.role: Published The Fault in Our Stars — cultural phenomenon, #1 NYT bestseller, major film
biography.lesson: Proved that teenagers thinking about death is not depressing — it is the most serious literature there is. YA is not a lesser genre.

biography.role: Co-founded Crash Course with Hank — educational YouTube
biography.lesson: Education scales when it is built on genuine enthusiasm, not institutional authority. Now 17M+ subscribers.

biography.role: Published Turtles All the Way Down — first novel directly about OCD
biography.lesson: Went public with his mental illness. Naming the thing is the first step. 'There is hope, even if your brain tells you there isn't.'

biography.role: Created The Anthropocene Reviewed podcast and book — 36 episodes, 44 essays, #1 NYT
biography.lesson: The five-star review as philosophical method. Pay attention to anything long enough and it becomes a portal to meaning.

biography.role: Published Everything is Tuberculosis — #1 NYT nonfiction bestseller
biography.lesson: Moral nonfiction: the argument that indifference, not malice, is humanity's deadliest force. Hope as action, not feeling.

skills.name: advise
skills.title: Strategic Counsel
skills.desc: Recommendations grounded in John's frameworks on meaning, attention, and moral action
skills.example: How do I find meaning in work that feels insignificant?

skills.name: teach
skills.title: Explanations
skills.desc: Explain concepts through John's lens — hope, attention, community, imagining complexly
skills.example: Why does John believe hope is a discipline?

skills.name: debate
skills.title: Counterarguments
skills.desc: Steel-man the other side of John's positions — is hope naive? Is suffering redemptive?
skills.example: Is it naive to believe the world is getting better?

skills.name: connect
skills.title: Bridges
skills.desc: Find unexpected links between John's ideas across domains
skills.example: Connect attention to storytelling to mental health.

skills.name: evolve
skills.title: Timeline of Thought
skills.desc: Trace how John's thinking has changed from early novels through Anthropocene to TB
skills.example: How has his thinking on suffering evolved over 20 years?

skills.name: surprise
skills.title: Serendipity
skills.desc: Surface a high-quality atom you haven't encountered yet
skills.example: No input needed — just run it.

skills.name: coach
skills.title: Socratic Questions
skills.desc: Socratic questioning — no answers, just what John would ask you to ask yourself
skills.example: I feel paralyzed by the scale of the world's problems.

skills.name: predict
skills.title: Implication Chains
skills.desc: Cascade second and third-order effects through John's moral framework
skills.example: What happens to meaning-making as AI replaces human creative work?

youtube_sources.note: Vlogbrothers is a shared channel with Hank Green. ONLY extract atoms from videos where JOHN is speaking. John's videos are typically titled from John's perspective and posted on Tuesdays. Filter by speaker before extraction.

podcast_sources.name: The Anthropocene Reviewed
podcast_sources.note: Primary source. Each episode reviews 2 topics on a 5-star scale. Full transcripts on Nerdfighteria Wiki. All episodes are JOHN only (no Hank).

podcast_sources.name: NPR Wild Card with Rachel Martin
podcast_sources.note: Hope, despair, TB, faith — broad personal philosophy interview.

podcast_sources.name: Freakonomics — The Deadliest Disease in Human History
podcast_sources.note: Deep TB discussion. Levitt says this is John's 'life's calling.' ~60 min.

podcast_sources.name: Chris Hayes — Why Is This Happening?
podcast_sources.note: 55 min. Hope, despair, global health, whether the world is ending.

podcast_sources.name: 99% Invisible — Everything Is Tuberculosis
podcast_sources.note: TB deep dive with Roman Mars.

podcast_sources.name: The Happiness Lab
podcast_sources.note: Hope as discipline, not feeling. Most 'punk rock' thing is to tackle problems head-on.

podcast_sources.name: The Daily (NYT) — The Interview
podcast_sources.note: 44 min. Broad career and philosophy interview.

podcast_sources.name: NPR Fresh Air — OCD interview
podcast_sources.note: Key OCD/mental health interview around Turtles All the Way Down.

speech_sources.name: TED — The nerd's guide to learning everything online
speech_sources.note: Education, online community, learning as social practice.

speech_sources.name: TEDxIndianapolis — Paper Towns and Why Learning is Awesome
speech_sources.note: Cartography as metaphor. Paper towns = fictions we build about places and people.

speech_sources.name: Kenyon College Commencement
speech_sources.note: Liberal arts education teaches listening. His alma mater (BA English + Religious Studies).

speech_sources.name: Butler University Commencement
speech_sources.note: Story about a Kuwaiti friend named Hassan. Empathy, perspective, war.

book_sources.title: Looking for Alaska
themes: the search for the Great Perhaps

book_sources.title: The Fault in Our Stars
themes: the fault is that we exist

book_sources.title: Turtles All the Way Down
themes: you are not your intrusive thoughts

website.tagline: Hope, attention, and the meaning we make in the Anthropocene
website.bio: Novelist, essayist, and Vlogbrother. Author of The Fault in Our Stars, The Anthropocene Reviewed, and Everything is Tuberculosis. Two decades of thinking publicly about hope, suffering, attention, and the beauty of ordinary things.
